title: Portuguese custard tarts
subtitle: Pastéis de nata with blistered custard and flaky pastry cups
excerpt: Rough puff pastry cases pressed into deep muffin tins and filled with a sugar-syrup custard baked at high heat until the custard blisters and chars in patches. The Belém original, made domestically, requires a very hot oven and a quick-set custard.
sourceNotes: Pastéis de nata (literally 'cream pastries') originated at the Jerónimos Monastery in Belém, Lisbon, in the early nineteenth century, made by monks who used egg whites for starching clothes and developed the custard tarts to use the leftover yolks. The recipe was sold to a nearby sugar refinery in 1837 and is still made at the Pastéis de Belém bakery. The domestic recipe here follows the standard method of a quick-cooked sugar-and-milk custard thickened with cornflour and egg yolks, baked at very high heat (250°C or higher) to develop the characteristic dark blisters.

content.text: The blistered surface that identifies a proper pastel de nata comes from baking at the highest temperature your oven can reach. The custard, set at 250°C or above, chars in patches while the centre stays liquid: the carryover heat finishes it in the tin. Using
rather than shortcrust gives the flaky, multi-layered cups the recipe is known for. An oven thermometer is worth using: domestic ovens rarely reach 250°C accurately.

Roll the rough puff into a tight cylinder before slicing: the slice pressed into the muffin cup creates the spiral pastry base seen in the originals. Pressing the disc into the cup rather than rolling it out separately is what achieves the right casing thickness.
Make the rough puff and form the cases

Mix the {{plain-flour}}, {{fine-salt}}, and cold {{unsalted-butter}} cubes together until the butter is in flat, pea-sized pieces coated in flour. Add 80-90 ml of cold water and bring together into a shaggy dough: the butter should remain in pieces. Roll out on a floured surface into a rectangle about 20 x 40 cm. Fold into thirds, rotate 90°, and roll out again. Repeat twice more (3 folds total). Roll into a tight cylinder about 25 cm long. Wrap and chill for 30 minutes. Slice the cylinder into 12 equal rounds. Press each round into the cups of an ungreased 12-hole muffin tin, pushing up the sides with your thumbs to form a case. Chill while you make the custard.

Heat the oven to 250°C (230°C fan), or the highest your oven allows. Put the {{caster-sugar}}, 60 ml of water, and {{cinnamon-stick}} in a small saucepan. Bring to a boil and cook for 3 minutes without stirring to make a light syrup; remove the cinnamon stick. In a separate medium saucepan, whisk together the {{cornflour}} and a few tablespoons of the {{whole-milk}} until smooth, then whisk in the remaining milk. Bring to a simmer over medium heat, stirring constantly, until thickened (about 3 minutes). Remove from the heat. Whisk together the {{egg-yolks}}, {{double-cream}}, and {{vanilla-extract}}, then whisk in the hot sugar syrup, then the hot milk mixture. The custard will be fairly liquid.

Fill each pastry case to three-quarters full with the warm custard. Bake at the highest oven temperature for 15-18 minutes until the pastry is deep golden and the custard surface is blistered and charred in patches. The custard will puff and wobble: this is correct. Leave to cool in the tin for 10 minutes, then remove. Eat warm, dusted with cinnamon.
